annotations.label: Projective geometry (non-metrical)

anchor.snippet: Projective geometry is an elementary non- metrical form of geometry

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the projectivization of a vector space, which provides a model of projective geometry, but does not articulate the broad non-metrical informal notion.

annotations.label: Duality in the projective plane

anchor.snippet: The simplest illustration of duality is in the projective plane

annotations.note: The `ProjectivePlane (Dual L) (Dual P)` instance in Combinatorics/Configuration.lean witnesses point–line duality for projective planes.

annotations.label: Projective range

anchor.snippet: A projective range is the one-dimensional foundation

annotations.note: Mathlib has the projectivization of an arbitrary vector space (including 2-dim, giving a projective line) but no dedicated `ProjectiveRange` notion.

annotations.label: Desargues' Theorem and Pappus's hexagon theorem

anchor.snippet: Additional properties of fundamental importance include Desargues' Theorem

annotations.note: No occurrence of `Desargues` or `Pappus` in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Line-field correspondence with point at infinity

anchor.snippet: the points of each line are in one-to-one correspondence with a given field

annotations.note: Mathlib has no statement giving an explicit bijection between a projective line and `K ∪ {∞}` (only the abstract `ℙ K (Fin 2 → K)` construction).

annotations.label: Fano plane PG(2,2)

anchor.snippet: The smallest 2-dimensional projective geometry (that with the fewest points) is the Fano plane

annotations.note: Grep for `Fano` returns no hits in Mathlib; only the generic `ProjectivePlane (ℙ K (Fin 3 → K))` instance over a field exists.

annotations.label: PG(a,b) notation for finite projective geometry

anchor.snippet: In standard notation, a finite projective geometry is written PG

annotations.note: Mathlib's `ℙ K V` covers the construction underlying PG(n,q) (taking `K = GF(q)` and `V = K^{n+1}`), but no dedicated PG(a,b) name/notation.

annotations.label: Elliptic incidence property

anchor.snippet: The fundamental property that singles out all projective geometries is the elliptic incidence property

annotations.note: The `HasPoints` typeclass (extended by `ProjectivePlane`) directly axiomatizes that every two distinct lines meet in a point.

annotations.label: Parallel postulates of elliptic, Euclidean, hyperbolic geometry

anchor.snippet: The parallel properties of elliptic, Euclidean and hyperbolic geometries contrast as follows

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize the trichotomy of parallel postulates across elliptic/Euclidean/hyperbolic geometries.

annotations.label: Principle of duality (Gergonne)

anchor.snippet: Joseph Gergonne noted the principle of duality characterizing projective plane geometry

annotations.note: The duality instance shows that `Dual L`, `Dual P` is again a projective plane, formalizing Gergonne's duality principle.

annotations.label: Duality in 3 dimensions (points and planes)

anchor.snippet: Similarly in 3 dimensions, the duality relation holds between points and planes

annotations.note: Mathlib's duality results are for projective planes (Combinatorics/Configuration), not 3-dimensional point–plane duality.

annotations.label: Pascal's theorem

anchor.snippet: If all six vertices of a hexagon lie on a conic

annotations.note: Grep for `Pascal` finds only combinatorial identities (Vandermonde, choose sums); no projective Pascal's theorem in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Brianchon's theorem

anchor.snippet: If all six sides of a hexagon are tangent to a conic

annotations.note: No `Brianchon` occurrences in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Elliptic parallel axiom

anchor.snippet: Projective geometries are characterised by the

annotations.note: Same content as the elliptic incidence property: `HasPoints` requires every two lines to share a point.

annotations.label: Whitehead's axioms G1, G2, G3

anchor.snippet: These axioms are based on Whitehead

annotations.note: Mathlib's `ProjectivePlane` class captures the spirit of Whitehead's axioms but is not labeled or split into G1/G2/G3.

annotations.label: Ternary collinearity axioms C0–C3

anchor.snippet: One can pursue axiomatization by postulating a ternary relation

annotations.note: No ternary-collinearity axiomatization of projective space exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Line via ternary relation

anchor.snippet: For two distinct points, A and B, the line AB is defined as consisting of all points C

annotations.note: Lines are not defined via a ternary collinearity relation anywhere in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Subspace defined recursively

anchor.snippet: may thus be recursively defined in terms of the subspace

annotations.note: Mathlib defines projective `Subspace` via closure under projective addition (not by Wikipedia's recursive line-extension scheme).

annotations.label: Independence of a point set

anchor.snippet: is a minimal generating subset for the subspace

annotations.note: `Projectivization.Independent` formalizes independence of a family of projective points via linear independence of representatives.

annotations.label: Minimum dimension axioms L1–L4

anchor.snippet: The minimum dimension is determined by the existence of an independent set of the required size

annotations.note: No L1–L4 minimum-dimension axiom list in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Maximum dimension axioms M1–M3

anchor.snippet: The maximum dimension may also be determined in a similar fashion

annotations.note: No M1–M3 maximum-dimension axiom list in Mathlib.

annotations.label: All coplanar lines intersect

anchor.snippet: all coplanar lines intersect

annotations.note: `HasPoints` gives that any two lines (in the projective plane) intersect, capturing the coplanar case but not phrased relative to a coplanarity hypothesis.

annotations.label: Axioms P1, P2, P3 for projective planes

anchor.snippet: An axiom system that achieves this is as follows

annotations.note: `ProjectivePlane` bundles the line-through-two-points, point-of-two-lines, and three-points-in-general-position axioms — matching P1/P2/P3.

annotations.label: Harmonic quadruple of points

anchor.snippet: An harmonic quadruple of points on a line occurs when there is a complete quadrangle

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of a harmonic conjugate / harmonic quadruple in projective geometry.

annotations.label: Harmonic quadruples preserved by perspectivity

anchor.snippet: Thus harmonic quadruples are preserved by perspectivity

annotations.note: Neither harmonic quadruples nor perspectivities are defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Projectivity as composition of perspectivities

anchor.snippet: The composition of two perspectivities is no longer a perspectivity, but a projectivity

annotations.note: Grep for `perspectivity`/`projectivity` returns no projective-geometry definitions in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Steiner conic (projective conic)

anchor.snippet: The set of such intersections is called a projective conic

annotations.note: Mathlib has no `Steiner` definition and no synthetic projective conic; `Conic`-named files in Mathlib refer to category-theoretic conical limits, not conics.

annotations.label: Pole and polar of a point with respect to a conic

anchor.snippet: The line through the other two diagonal points is called the polar of P

annotations.note: No pole/polar construction with respect to a conic is present in Mathlib.
